tech_stack.framework: React 18 + TypeScript + Vite (frontend), Node.js + Express + TypeScript (backend)
tech_stack.auth: JWT tokens with bcrypt password hashing

structural_rules: Service layer access only â€” no direct DB calls from UI or route handlers
Boundary validation at every entry point (API routes, form inputs)
Separation of concerns: data / logic / presentation
Clear separation of routes, controllers, services (per spec quality criteria)
assumptions: Monorepo structure with shared root package.json â€” Vite dev server + Express API server
SQLite file-based DB chosen for zero-setup local development
No hosting specified â€” app is local-first for now
No boilerplate covers any mechanism category â€” all 14 need explicit implementation
Spec covers categories A-J explicitly; K-N not mentioned and may not apply to this app

stage_1.raw_input: Build a Team Task Manager app. Read the full spec at C:\Users\lober\.archon\.archon\test-specs\task-manager-benchmark.md Tech stack: React 18, TypeScript, Vite, Tailwind CSS frontend. Node.js Express TypeScript backend. SQLite via Prisma ORM. JWT auth with bcrypt. Vitest for testing.

archetype_matches.rationale: Core of the app is task CRUD â€” create, read, update, delete tasks with forms and lists
Sprint 3 adds a dashboard with task counts by status, overdue highlighting, and aggregation

mechanisms_identified.name: Data Input
mechanisms_identified.evidence: Registration form, login form, task creation/edit form, team creation form, add-member form
mechanisms_identified.spec_ref: Sprint 1-2 features + API endpoints POST /auth/register, POST /tasks, POST /teams

mechanisms_identified.name: Data Storage
mechanisms_identified.evidence: Prisma ORM + SQLite with 4 models: User, Team, TeamMember, Task. File-based persistence explicitly required.
mechanisms_identified.spec_ref: Data Model section + pass criteria: 'SQLite database file is created and persists data'

mechanisms_identified.name: Data Processing
mechanisms_identified.evidence: Input validation (email format, required fields, enum values for status/priority), dashboard aggregation (task counts, overdue calculation), status transition logic
mechanisms_identified.spec_ref: Quality criteria: 'rejects malformed data with helpful messages' + Sprint 3 dashboard + Sprint 2 status workflow

mechanisms_identified.name: Data Output
mechanisms_identified.evidence: Task lists with filters, dashboard stats, sorted views, responsive layout (mobile 375px + desktop)
mechanisms_identified.spec_ref: Sprint 3 features + Quality criteria: 'usable on 375px mobile width'

mechanisms_identified.name: Authentication
mechanisms_identified.evidence: JWT token issue/verify, bcrypt password hashing, register/login/me endpoints, protected route rejection
mechanisms_identified.spec_ref: Sprint 1 + API auth endpoints + pass criteria: 'Protected routes reject requests without valid JWT'

mechanisms_identified.name: Authorization
mechanisms_identified.evidence: Team roles (OWNER|MEMBER), task assignment permissions, team ownership checks
mechanisms_identified.spec_ref: Sprint 2 + TeamMember.role field + Quality criteria: error codes 401, 403

mechanisms_identified.name: Communication
mechanisms_identified.evidence: No notifications, email, push, or messaging mentioned in spec. App is a task tracker, not a communication tool.
mechanisms_identified.spec_ref: Not referenced in any sprint or API endpoint

mechanisms_identified.name: Integration
mechanisms_identified.evidence: Self-contained app. No external service connections, payment gateways, or third-party APIs.
mechanisms_identified.spec_ref: Not referenced. REST API is internal between frontend and backend.

mechanisms_identified.name: Workflow
mechanisms_identified.evidence: Task status state machine: TODO â†’ IN_PROGRESS â†’ REVIEW â†’ DONE. Explicit transition enforcement.
mechanisms_identified.spec_ref: Sprint 2: 'Task status workflow: TODO â†’ IN_PROGRESS â†’ REVIEW â†’ DONE'

mechanisms_identified.name: Search & Discovery
mechanisms_identified.evidence: Multi-parameter task filtering (assignee, status, priority, due date) + sorting (priority, due date, created date)
mechanisms_identified.spec_ref: Sprint 2 filters + Sprint 3 sorting + GET /api/tasks with filters

mechanisms_identified.name: Collaboration
mechanisms_identified.evidence: Team membership and task assignment are lightweight collaboration. No comments, @mentions, reactions, or activity feeds in spec.
mechanisms_identified.spec_ref: Sprint 2 team features â€” implicit collaboration through shared task boards

mechanisms_identified.name: Monetization
mechanisms_identified.evidence: No pricing, subscriptions, or payment mentioned. This is an internal tool / benchmark app.

mechanisms_identified.name: Admin/Ops
mechanisms_identified.evidence: No admin dashboard, user management panel, or moderation tools mentioned.

mechanisms_identified.name: Infrastructure
mechanisms_identified.evidence: Prisma handles DB migrations. Vitest handles testing. No explicit caching, monitoring, CI/CD, or logging requirements.

gap_fills.gap: Collaboration features beyond team membership not specified

gap_fills.assumption: Team task assignment satisfies the collaboration need for this benchmark app

gap_fills.gap: No explicit infrastructure requirements beyond Prisma + Vitest
gap_fills.fill: Prisma migrations for schema changes. Vitest for test runner. Console-level logging only. No CI/CD pipeline.
gap_fills.assumption: Local development environment â€” no production infrastructure needed for benchmark

competitors.name: Trello
competitors.positioning: Kanban-first, simplicity-focused. Best for small teams (2-5 people). Free tier is generous.
competitors.relevant_pattern: Visual board metaphor with drag-and-drop cards. Our app uses a simpler list/filter approach.

competitors.name: Asana
competitors.positioning: Full project management for large teams (200+). Multiple views: list, board, timeline, calendar.
competitors.relevant_pattern: Task dependencies, milestones, workload management. Our scope is deliberately smaller â€” CRUD + filters + dashboard.

competitors.name: Linear
competitors.positioning: Developer-focused, keyboard-driven, fast. Opinionated workflow with cycles and projects.
competitors.relevant_pattern: Clean UI, status-based workflow (similar to our TODOâ†’DONE flow), team-centric organization.

competitors.name: Monday.com / Basecamp
competitors.positioning: Monday is highly visual and customizable. Basecamp bundles project management + communication.
competitors.relevant_pattern: Both emphasize team dashboards and status tracking â€” validates our Sprint 3 dashboard feature.

patterns: All major task managers use status-based workflows (Kanban columns = status values)
Filtering by assignee + status + priority is table stakes â€” every competitor has it
Dashboard with aggregated stats is a premium/differentiator feature in simpler tools (Trello lacks it)
Team-based organization with role permissions (owner/member/viewer) is universal
REST API architecture with JWT auth is the standard pattern for React+Express task apps (confirmed by open source examples)
differentiators: This is a benchmark app, not a market competitor â€” focus is on clean architecture over feature breadth
SQLite + Prisma gives zero-setup local dev, unlike competitors requiring cloud DBs
Deliberately scoped to 3 sprints â€” avoids feature creep that plagues most task manager builds

in_scope: Project scaffolding â€” Vite + Express monorepo with TypeScript
Database schema â€” 4 models (User, Team, TeamMember, Task) via Prisma + SQLite
User registration and login with JWT + bcrypt
Full task CRUD (create, read, update, delete) with API + UI
Team creation, joining, and member management with OWNER/MEMBER roles
Task filtering by assignee, status, priority, due date
Task status workflow: TODO â†’ IN_PROGRESS â†’ REVIEW â†’ DONE
Dashboard with task counts by status and overdue highlighting
Task sorting by priority, due date, created date
Input validation with proper error codes (400, 401, 403, 404, 500)
Vitest tests â€” at least 1 test per API endpoint
TypeScript throughout â€” no any types, proper interfaces
not_in_scope: Notifications (email, push, in-app) â€” no communication mechanism
Admin panel or user management dashboard
OAuth / SSO / MFA â€” JWT email/password only
Real-time updates (WebSocket) â€” REST polling only
deferred: Task comments and activity feed (natural collaboration extension)
Kanban board view (visual alternative to list view)
Email notifications for task assignment / due date reminders

stage_2.completeness_breakdown: Required categories (A,B,C,D,E,F,I,J) = 8/8 covered (70 pts). Optional categories: K implied, N implied, G/H/L/M not applicable. 2/2 applicable optional partially resolved (15 pts). Total: 85/100.
stage_2.combined_raw: Build a Team Task Manager app â€” a team task management web app where users can create accounts, manage tasks, assign them to team members, and track progress. Tech stack: React 18 + TypeScript + Vite + Tailwind CSS frontend, Node.js + Express + TypeScript backend, SQLite via Prisma ORM, JWT auth with bcrypt, Vitest testing. 3 sprints: (1) scaffolding + schema + auth + task CRUD, (2) teams + assignment + filtering + status workflow, (3) dashboard + overdue + sorting + responsive. 4 data models: User, Team, TeamMember, Task. 11 REST API endpoints across auth, teams, and tasks. Status workflow: TODOâ†’IN_PROGRESSâ†’REVIEWâ†’DONE. Filtering by assignee/status/priority/due_date. Dashboard with counts + overdue highlighting. Gap fills: collaboration scoped to team assignment only (no comments), infrastructure scoped to Prisma migrations + Vitest (no CI/CD). Market context: architecture aligns with standard React+Express task manager patterns; scope is deliberately smaller than Asana/Linear/Trello, focusing on clean architecture for benchmarking.

user_input: Build a Team Task Manager app. Read the full spec at C:\Users\lober\.archon\.archon\test-specs\task-manager-benchmark.md Tech stack: React 18, TypeScript, Vite, Tailwind CSS frontend. Node.js Express TypeScript backend. SQLite via Prisma ORM. JWT auth with bcrypt. Vitest for testing.

concept_and_context.one_line_description: A team-based task management web app where users create accounts, organize into teams, assign tasks, track status workflows, and monitor progress via a dashboard.
concept_and_context.product_identity: A benchmark-quality full-stack CRUD application demonstrating clean architecture patterns with React 18 + Express + TypeScript + Prisma. Not a market competitor â€” a reference implementation proving competence across auth, team collaboration, task workflows, and responsive UI.
concept_and_context.core_value_proposition: Provides a complete, working team task management system with zero-setup local development (SQLite), proper role-based access (OWNER/MEMBER), structured task workflows (TODOâ†’IN_PROGRESSâ†’REVIEWâ†’DONE), and a dashboard for progress visibility â€” all built to benchmark-grade code quality standards.

personas.name: Dana â€” Engineering Team Lead
pain_points: Needs to see who is working on what at a glance
Tasks get stuck in ambiguous states â€” no enforced workflow
Existing tools (spreadsheets, Slack threads) lose track of assignments
Overdue tasks go unnoticed until standup meetings
goals: Assign tasks to team members with clear ownership
Track task status through a defined workflow
Dashboard showing overdue and blocked work
personas.evidence_basis: INFERENCE â€” needs validation. Derived from spec features (team roles, assignment, dashboard, overdue highlighting) which directly address team lead visibility needs.

personas.name: Marcus â€” Individual Contributor
personas.role: Developer or team member working on assigned tasks
pain_points: Unclear what to work on next â€” no priority sorting
Status updates require manual communication instead of self-service
Filtering to 'my tasks' is clunky in general-purpose tools
goals: See assigned tasks filtered and sorted by priority/due date
Update task status through a clear workflow
Mobile-friendly access for quick status checks
personas.evidence_basis: INFERENCE â€” needs validation. Derived from spec features (assignee filtering, status workflow, priority sorting, 375px responsive layout).

personas.name: Alex â€” Benchmark Evaluator
personas.role: Technical evaluator assessing code quality of the built app
pain_points: Most benchmark apps cut corners on auth, validation, or testing
Architecture quality is hard to assess without clear separation of concerns
Apps claiming full-stack often skip error handling or responsive design
goals: Evaluate clean architecture: routes â†’ controllers â†’ services â†’ DB
Verify proper auth (JWT + bcrypt), validation (error codes), and testing (Vitest)
Assess TypeScript strictness â€” no 'any' types
personas.evidence_basis: Explicit in spec. The app is described as a benchmark with specific quality criteria and pass/fail conditions.

target_user_and_market.market_context: The task management market is dominated by established players (Trello, Asana, Linear, Monday.com, Basecamp) each targeting different segments. Trello owns simplicity/kanban for small teams. Asana and Monday.com serve enterprise with rich views and integrations. Linear targets developers with speed and keyboard-driven UX. All competitors confirm that status-based workflows, assignee/priority filtering, and team role permissions are table-stakes features. Dashboard aggregation (task counts, overdue) is a differentiator in simpler tools â€” Trello lacks it natively. Source: stage_2.market_research.patterns and competitor analysis.
target_user_and_market.competitive_landscape: This product is NOT competing in the task management market. It is a benchmark/reference implementation. Its competitive set is other open-source task manager tutorials and full-stack demo apps on GitHub. In that context, it differentiates through: (1) zero-setup local dev via SQLite + Prisma, (2) deliberate 3-sprint scope that avoids feature creep, (3) enforced code quality standards (TypeScript strict, Vitest coverage, proper error codes). Source: stage_2.market_research.differentiators.

feasibility.viability_summary: HIGH VIABILITY. Every feature in scope uses well-established patterns with mature tooling. React 18 + Express + Prisma + SQLite is a proven stack with extensive documentation and community support. JWT auth with bcrypt is the standard pattern for this class of app. All 14 mechanism categories are either covered by explicit spec features (8 of 14) or correctly scoped out (4 not applicable, 2 implied with minimal gap fills). No novel technical challenges â€” this is assembly of known patterns, not invention.

risks.risk: Prisma + SQLite edge cases in concurrent writes
risks.mitigation: SQLite WAL mode + Prisma's built-in retry logic. Single-server local app means low concurrency. Not a production deployment.
risks.evidence_basis: market_research â€” open-source task manager repos on GitHub commonly use this stack without reported concurrency issues for local dev.

risks.risk: Task status workflow enforcement may be underspecified
risks.mitigation: Spec says TODOâ†’IN_PROGRESSâ†’REVIEWâ†’DONE but doesn't clarify if backward transitions are allowed or if skipping states is permitted. Decision: enforce forward-only transitions as the stricter interpretation. Log this as a decision.
risks.evidence_basis: INFERENCE â€” spec says 'Task status workflow: TODO â†’ IN_PROGRESS â†’ REVIEW â†’ DONE' with arrows suggesting forward-only, but doesn't explicitly forbid backward transitions.

risks.risk: Responsive layout at 375px with complex filter/sort UI
risks.mitigation: Tailwind CSS responsive utilities handle this natively. Filters can collapse into a dropdown/drawer on mobile. Competitors (Trello, Linear) solve this with simplified mobile views.
risks.evidence_basis: market_research â€” all major competitors offer responsive/mobile layouts. Tailwind's responsive system is well-documented for this use case.

risks.risk: Test coverage target unclear â€” 'at least 1 test per API endpoint' may be insufficient
risks.mitigation: Spec explicitly states minimum: 1 test per endpoint. Vitest + supertest is the standard Express testing pattern. Scope to spec requirement â€” don't gold-plate.

feasibility.evidence_basis: Viability assessment based on: (1) market_research confirming stack alignment with standard patterns, (2) stage_2 mechanism coverage at 85/100, (3) all features using mature, well-documented libraries.

stage_3.problem_statement: Small teams managing tasks through informal channels (Slack, email, spreadsheets) lose visibility into who owns what, what's overdue, and where work is stuck. Without a structured system: (1) task ownership is ambiguous â€” work falls through cracks or gets duplicated, (2) status is communicated ad-hoc â€” managers learn about blockers too late, (3) priority and due dates aren't enforced â€” urgent work competes with low-priority tasks without clear sorting, (4) progress is invisible â€” no dashboard means status requires manual aggregation. This product addresses each pain point directly: team membership with roles establishes ownership, the TODOâ†’DONE workflow enforces status tracking, filtering/sorting by priority and due date surfaces what matters, and the dashboard with overdue highlighting makes progress (and stalls) visible at a glance. As a benchmark app, it also solves the meta-problem: most full-stack demo apps sacrifice architecture quality for feature count. This app deliberately scopes to 3 sprints and prioritizes clean code over breadth.
stage_3.drift_anchor: Team Task Manager is a benchmark-quality full-stack web app for team-based task management. Users register, form teams with OWNER/MEMBER roles, create and assign tasks, track them through a TODOâ†’IN_PROGRESSâ†’REVIEWâ†’DONE workflow, filter/sort by assignee/status/priority/due date, and monitor progress on a dashboard with overdue highlighting. It is a reference implementation â€” not a market product â€” built with React 18 + Express + TypeScript + Prisma + SQLite to demonstrate clean architecture across auth, CRUD, workflows, and responsive UI.

decision_log.decision: Product identity: benchmark app, not market competitor
decision_log.choice: Position as reference implementation focused on code quality
decision_log.alternatives_considered: Position as MVP for actual team use; position as learning tutorial
decision_log.rationale: Spec explicitly frames this as a benchmark with pass/fail quality criteria. Market research confirms the task management space is saturated â€” competing would require 10x the scope. Benchmark framing keeps scope controlled.

decision_log.choice: Enforce TODOâ†’IN_PROGRESSâ†’REVIEWâ†’DONE as forward-only (no backward transitions)
decision_log.alternatives_considered: Allow any-to-any transitions; allow backward transitions with confirmation; allow skip-forward but not backward
decision_log.rationale: Spec uses directional arrows (â†’) suggesting forward progression. Forward-only is the stricter interpretation and aligns with Linear's opinionated workflow model (market research). Simpler to implement and test. If users need backward transitions, this decision is easily reversed.

decision_log.choice: No comments, @mentions, activity feeds, or real-time features
decision_log.alternatives_considered: Add basic task comments; add activity log per task; add real-time updates via WebSocket
decision_log.rationale: Stage 2 gap fill explicitly scoped collaboration to team-based assignment. Spec doesn't mention comments or activity feeds. Adding them would expand scope beyond the 3-sprint boundary. Deferred as natural extensions (stage_2.scope_contract.deferred).

decision_log.decision: Persona focus: three distinct user types
decision_log.choice: Team Lead (assigner/viewer), Individual Contributor (doer), Benchmark Evaluator (assessor)
decision_log.alternatives_considered: Single 'team member' persona; two personas (admin/user); four+ personas adding PM, designer, etc.
decision_log.rationale: Three personas map to the three distinct interaction patterns in the spec: (1) team/task management and dashboard viewing, (2) task execution and status updates, (3) code quality evaluation against pass criteria. More personas would be speculative without user research.

decision_log.choice: Prisma migrations + Vitest only. No hosting, monitoring, or deployment pipeline.
decision_log.alternatives_considered: Add Docker setup; add GitHub Actions CI; add basic logging middleware
decision_log.rationale: Stage 2 gap fill and scope contract explicitly exclude CI/CD and production deployment. SQLite file-based DB is chosen for zero-setup local dev. Adding infrastructure would conflict with the benchmark's simplicity goal.

decision_log.choice: Emphasize SQLite + Prisma zero-setup as key differentiator vs. open-source alternatives requiring Docker/PostgreSQL
decision_log.alternatives_considered: Emphasize TypeScript strictness; emphasize test coverage; emphasize sprint-based scope control
decision_log.rationale: Market research shows most open-source task manager repos require database setup (PostgreSQL, MongoDB). SQLite + Prisma eliminates this friction entirely. This is the most immediately tangible differentiator for anyone evaluating or running the benchmark.

ambiguity_resolutions.ambiguity: Task status transitions â€” can tasks move backward (e.g., DONEâ†’IN_PROGRESS)?
ambiguity_resolutions.resolution: Forward-only transitions enforced. Arrows in spec (â†’) indicate unidirectional flow.
ambiguity_resolutions.priority_rule_applied: Explicit spec interpretation â€” directional arrows treated as constraint.

ambiguity_resolutions.ambiguity: Collaboration depth â€” does 'team task management' imply comments, mentions, or activity feeds?
ambiguity_resolutions.resolution: Scoped to team membership + task assignment only. No comments, mentions, or activity feeds.
ambiguity_resolutions.priority_rule_applied: Stage 2 gap fill (scoped_down) â€” only features explicitly in spec are in scope.

ambiguity_resolutions.ambiguity: Dashboard scope â€” does 'task counts by status' include charts/graphs or just numbers?
ambiguity_resolutions.resolution: Numbers with overdue highlighting. No charts or graphs â€” spec says 'task counts' and 'overdue highlighting', not visualization.
ambiguity_resolutions.priority_rule_applied: Explicit spec language â€” 'counts' and 'highlighting' are specific terms, not 'charts' or 'visualization'.

ambiguity_resolutions.ambiguity: Test depth â€” '1 test per API endpoint' means happy path only or error cases too?
ambiguity_resolutions.resolution: Minimum: 1 happy-path test per endpoint. Spec sets this as a floor, not a ceiling. Error case tests are good practice but not a pass criterion.
ambiguity_resolutions.priority_rule_applied: Explicit spec language â€” 'at least 1 test per API endpoint' sets minimum, not maximum.

ambiguity_resolutions.ambiguity: Team roles â€” what can MEMBER do vs. OWNER beyond what's explicitly listed?
ambiguity_resolutions.resolution: OWNER: full CRUD on team + manage members + all task operations. MEMBER: task CRUD within team + status updates on assigned tasks. No further role granularity.
ambiguity_resolutions.priority_rule_applied: Spec mentions OWNER|MEMBER roles with team ownership checks. Inferred permissions from standard RBAC patterns aligned with market research (all competitors use similar owner/member splits).

mechanisms.name: Project Scaffolding
mechanisms.description: Monorepo setup with Vite React 18 frontend + Express TypeScript backend, shared tsconfig, dev scripts, and Prisma initialization. The structural foundation everything else is built on.
mechanisms.chosen_approach: Standard Vite + Express monorepo with shared root package.json, separate tsconfig for client/server, concurrently for dev server

mechanisms.name: Database Schema & Persistence
mechanisms.description: Prisma ORM with SQLite defining 4 models (User, Team, TeamMember, Task) with relations, enums for status/priority/role, and migration management.
mechanisms.chosen_approach: Prisma schema with SQLite provider, enum types for Status (TODO|IN_PROGRESS|REVIEW|DONE), Priority (LOW|MEDIUM|HIGH|URGENT), Role (OWNER|MEMBER), standard relational foreign keys

mechanisms.name: User Authentication
mechanisms.description: User registration and login system with JWT token issuance/verification, bcrypt password hashing, and auth middleware for protected routes.
mechanisms.chosen_approach: bcrypt for password hashing, jsonwebtoken for JWT issue/verify, Express middleware for route protection, /auth/register + /auth/login + /auth/me endpoints

mechanisms.name: Task CRUD Operations
mechanisms.description: Full create, read, update, delete operations for tasks with API endpoints and React forms/lists. The central data entity of the application.
mechanisms.chosen_approach: REST endpoints (GET/POST/PUT/DELETE /api/tasks), Prisma queries in service layer, React forms with controlled inputs, TanStack-style data fetching or useEffect + fetch

mechanisms.name: Team Management
mechanisms.description: Team creation, member addition/removal, team listing, and membership queries. Teams are the organizational unit that groups users and their tasks.
mechanisms.chosen_approach: TeamMember join table with role field, REST endpoints for team CRUD + member management, creator auto-assigned OWNER role

mechanisms.name: Role-Based Authorization
mechanisms.description: OWNER/MEMBER permission enforcement on team and task operations. OWNERs manage teams and members; MEMBERs operate on tasks within their teams.
mechanisms.chosen_approach: Middleware + service-layer permission checks querying TeamMember.role, returning 403 for unauthorized operations, 401 for unauthenticated

mechanisms.name: Task Status Workflow
mechanisms.description: Forward-only state machine enforcing TODOâ†’IN_PROGRESSâ†’REVIEWâ†’DONE transitions. Invalid transitions are rejected with appropriate error responses.
mechanisms.chosen_approach: Service layer with declarative transition map â€” define VALID_TRANSITIONS as a const object mapping each status to its allowed next status, validate in task update service before persisting. Auto-selected: leads by >15 points over alternatives.

approaches.name: Service Layer with Transition Map
approaches.description: Define a VALID_TRANSITIONS const map (e.g., {TODO: ['IN_PROGRESS'], IN_PROGRESS: ['REVIEW'], ...}), check currentâ†’next validity in task service, throw 400 on invalid transition.

approaches.name: Prisma Middleware Interceptor
approaches.description: Use Prisma middleware to intercept Task update operations, validate status transitions at the ORM level before writes reach the database.

approaches.name: SQLite Database Triggers
approaches.description: Enforce valid transitions via SQLite BEFORE UPDATE triggers that reject invalid status changes at the database level.

evaluation.selection_reason: Service Layer with Transition Map leads by 115 points over Prisma Middleware and 250 points over DB Triggers. Clean, testable, declarative, and idiomatic for Express + Prisma stack.

mechanisms.alternate_approach: Prisma Middleware Interceptor (770 points â€” 115 point gap)

mechanisms.name: Task Filtering & Sorting
mechanisms.description: Multi-parameter server-side filtering (assignee, status, priority, due date) and sorting (priority, due date, created date) on task list queries.
mechanisms.chosen_approach: Query parameters on GET /api/tasks mapped to Prisma where/orderBy clauses, React filter UI with controlled selects/inputs sending params to API

mechanisms.name: Progress Dashboard
mechanisms.description: Aggregated view showing task counts by status and overdue task highlighting. Provides at-a-glance progress visibility for team leads.
mechanisms.chosen_approach: Prisma groupBy for status counts, date comparison for overdue detection, dedicated dashboard API endpoint or computed from task list, simple card/stat UI layout

mechanisms.name: Responsive UI Shell
mechanisms.description: Application layout, navigation, and responsive design supporting 375px mobile and desktop breakpoints using Tailwind CSS utilities.
mechanisms.chosen_approach: Tailwind responsive classes (sm/md/lg breakpoints), mobile-first layout, collapsible nav/filters on small screens, flex/grid layout system

mechanisms.name: Input Validation & Error Handling
mechanisms.description: Cross-cutting validation of all API inputs and form data with structured error responses using proper HTTP status codes (400, 401, 403, 404, 500).
mechanisms.chosen_approach: Zod schema validation â€” define Zod schemas for each endpoint's request body, validate in route handlers or middleware, return structured error messages. Auto-selected: leads by >15 points over alternatives.

approaches.name: Zod Schema Validation
approaches.description: Define Zod schemas for each request payload, use z.parse() in route handlers or a validation middleware. TypeScript-native with automatic type inference from schemas.

approaches.name: express-validator Middleware
approaches.description: Use express-validator chain syntax in route definitions. Express-specific, middleware-based validation with built-in sanitizers.

approaches.name: Manual Validation Functions
approaches.description: Hand-written validation utility functions per endpoint. No external dependency, full control, but more code to maintain.

evaluation.selection_reason: Zod leads by 80 points over express-validator and 150 points over manual. TypeScript-native type inference aligns perfectly with the strict TypeScript requirement. Prisma + Zod is a well-established pairing in the ecosystem.

mechanisms.alternate_approach: express-validator Middleware (795 points â€” 80 point gap)

mechanisms.name: Test Suite
mechanisms.description: Vitest test configuration and at least 1 test per API endpoint covering happy-path scenarios with supertest for HTTP assertions.
mechanisms.chosen_approach: Vitest config with supertest for Express endpoint testing, test database isolation via separate SQLite file or in-memory, at least 1 happy-path test per endpoint

design_notes: Zero ROOMs: This is a benchmark app with a detailed spec. Every step either has one correct way (WALL) or a constrained set of valid approaches (DOOR). No step is truly open-ended.

High WALL ratio: Authentication, database operations, validation, and API endpoints are deterministic sequences. The spec is prescriptive enough that most steps have exactly one correct implementation.

DOORs concentrated in UI: The spec defines WHAT the UI must show but not HOW to lay it out. Render steps, filter controls, dashboard cards, and app shell all have multiple valid approaches within constraints.
One skippable step: URL param sync for filters (mech_008_step_10) is a UX enhancement, not a functional requirement. Filters work without URL persistence.

Structural rules applied as classification lens: single_responsibility and boundary_validation are the most frequently applied rules, reflecting the app's emphasis on clean architecture and input safety.

phases.name: Foundation
phases.description: Project scaffolding, database schema, input validation, responsive UI shell, and user authentication. Everything needed before any business logic can be built.

build_order.rationale: Root project config — everything depends on this
Shared TypeScript config base
Server-specific TS config extending root
Client-specific TS config extending root
DATABASE_URL and JWT_SECRET environment variables
All 4 models, 3 enums, relations — DB foundation for everything
Prisma client singleton — all services use this
Zod schemas for auth endpoints — validation layer used by routes
Registration, login, token verification business logic
JWT verification middleware for protected routes
POST /auth/register, POST /auth/login, GET /auth/me endpoints
Express app setup, middleware, route mounting, listen
HTML entry point for Vite
Vite config with React plugin and API proxy
React DOM render entry point
Fetch wrapper and auth API functions
Shared TypeScript interfaces for User, Task, Team, etc.
Auth state provider — token storage, login/logout, current user
App shell with routing, nav, responsive layout wrapper
Login form page
Registration form page
Responsive layout wrapper — nav, sidebar, content area for 375px+ breakpoints

phases.name: Core Features
phases.description: Task CRUD, team management, role-based authorization, and task status workflow. The business logic heart of the application.

build_order.rationale: Add Zod schemas for task and team endpoints
VALID_TRANSITIONS map for task status state machine — TODO→IN_PROGRESS→REVIEW→DONE
Task CRUD business logic with status transition enforcement
Team creation, member management, membership queries
OWNER/MEMBER role checks — middleware querying TeamMember.role
GET/POST/PUT/DELETE /api/tasks endpoints with auth + authorization
Team CRUD + member management endpoints
Mount task and team routers
Add task and team API functions
Task data fetching and mutation hooks
Team data fetching and mutation hooks
Task list view with status badges
Create/edit task form with assignee, priority, due date, team selection
Team list and creation
Team members view with add/remove member
Task card component with status, priority, assignee display
Color-coded status badge with workflow transition buttons
Team member list with role indicators
Add routes for task and team pages

phases.name: Discovery, Dashboard & Testing
phases.description: Task filtering and sorting, progress dashboard with overdue highlighting, and Vitest test suite covering all API endpoints.

build_order.rationale: Add filtering (assignee, status, priority, due date) and sorting (priority, due date, created) to task queries
Prisma groupBy for status counts, date comparison for overdue detection
Wire query params to service filtering/sorting
GET /api/dashboard endpoint returning counts + overdue tasks
Mount dashboard router
Add dashboard API function and filter params to task fetch
Add filter/sort state and query param generation
Dashboard data fetching hook
Filter controls — assignee select, status select, priority select, due date picker
Sort toggle — priority, due date, created date with direction
Integrate filter/sort controls into task list
Status count cards + overdue task list with highlighting
Individual status count card component
Overdue tasks list with red highlighting
Add dashboard route
Vitest configuration with test database setup
Test database initialization and cleanup utilities
Tests for POST /auth/register, POST /auth/login, GET /auth/me
Tests for GET/POST/PUT/DELETE /api/tasks with auth
Tests for team CRUD and member management endpoints

low.trigger: Added or modified client/src/lib/types.ts beyond auth-related types
low.response: Log: types file may contain forward declarations for Phase 2 entities — acceptable if additive only

medium.trigger: Modified a Phase 2 or Phase 3 file (e.g., task.service.ts, dashboard.ts)
medium.response: Review: if additive (stub/placeholder), proceed with caution. If substantive logic, revert the file.

high.trigger: Deleted any existing file or removed exports from shared modules
high.response: Revert entire phase — deletion in foundation phase is destructive

high.trigger: Modified prisma/schema.prisma in a way that drops columns or tables
high.response: Revert entire phase — schema must be additive

critical.trigger: Modified .env to remove DATABASE_URL or JWT_SECRET
critical.response: FULL STOP — human review required. Environment variables are critical infrastructure.
FULL STOP — human review required. Build configuration files are immutable during build.

low.trigger: Added test helper utilities beyond setup.ts
low.response: Log: additional test helpers are acceptable if they support test clarity

medium.trigger: Modified a Phase 1 or Phase 2 read-only file
medium.response: Review: if fixing a bug found during testing, document the bug and fix. If refactoring, revert.

high.trigger: Deleted any existing file or removed Phase 1/2 exports
high.response: Revert entire phase — Phase 3 must not break existing contracts

high.trigger: Test files import and execute production code that modifies the dev database
high.response: Revert test file — tests must use isolated test database, never dev.db

critical.trigger: Tests pass by mocking the database instead of using a real test database
critical.response: FULL STOP — review required. Spec requires integration-level testing with real database.

two_strike_rule.rationale: If 2 fresh agents fail the same phase, the problem is the phase spec, not the agents.

step_a_self_report.description: Builder agent lists every file created or modified during the phase.
step_a_self_report.output: List of files with create/modify classification

step_b_diff_check.description: Run git diff against baseline snapshot to get ground-truth file changes.
validations: Every file in diff output must appear in self-report (mismatch = builder omitted a file)
Every file in self-report must appear in diff output (mismatch = builder hallucinated a file)
Every file in diff output must be in files_allowed list (unauthorized = sandbox violation)
No file in files_read_only may appear in diff output (read-only violation)
No file matching files_forbidden glob may appear in diff output (forbidden zone violation)

step_c_violation_response.description: Apply stage_8 violation decision tree based on diff findings.

decision_tree.self_report_mismatch_only: LOW — builder forgot to list a file but file is in allowed set
decision_tree.extra_file_in_allowed_set: LOW — additive file not in build_order but within sandbox
decision_tree.read_only_file_modified_minor: MEDIUM — review the diff; if bug fix, document and proceed
decision_tree.future_phase_file_touched_stub: MEDIUM — if additive placeholder only, proceed with caution
decision_tree.read_only_file_modified_major: HIGH — revert phase via git reset --hard ${BASELINE_SHA}
decision_tree.env_or_build_config_modified: CRITICAL — full stop for human review
decision_tree.auth_middleware_behavior_changed: CRITICAL — full stop for human review
decision_tree.tests_mock_db_instead_of_real: CRITICAL — full stop for human review

step_d_functional_checks.description: Run compile, test, and functional checks from stage_8 full_checkpoint.
step_d_functional_checks.on_any_failure: Classify as HIGH if compile fails, MEDIUM if single functional check fails, HIGH if multiple functional checks fail

classification_actions.LOW: Log finding, proceed to next phase
classification_actions.MEDIUM: Log finding with details, proceed to next phase with builder warned
classification_actions.HIGH: git reset --hard ${BASELINE_SHA}, retry phase with fresh builder context
classification_actions.CRITICAL: git reset --hard ${BASELINE_SHA}, stop for human review — do NOT retry

cumulative_state.escalation_rule: If 3+ MEDIUM violations accumulate across phases, escalate next MEDIUM to HIGH

manual_fallback_config.description: For non-CLI platforms: Phase N+1 opens with Phase N validation as a preamble. Agent B checks are inlined as the first task before builder work begins.
manual_fallback_config.preamble_template: Before starting Phase ${N+1}, verify Phase ${N} output: (1) List all files changed since baseline ${BASELINE_SHA}. (2) Confirm each is in files_allowed. (3) Run compile check. (4) Run functional checks. Report classification: CLEAN/LOW/MEDIUM/HIGH/CRITICAL. If HIGH or CRITICAL, STOP — do not proceed.
manual_fallback_config.fallback_trigger: Agent B process unavailable or platform does not support parallel agent execution

validation_details.import_references_accounted: Seam checks in each phase verify all cross-file imports

codebase_plan.enrichment_source: Greptacular React frontend (production code) + BUILD_RULES.md canonical patterns
codebase_plan.confidence_breakdown: Backend patterns (Express/Prisma/JWT) derived from BUILD_RULES.md canonical examples — well-specified but not from live code (85%). Frontend patterns (React hooks, fetch wrapper, Vite config, Tailwind) extracted from production Greptacular codebase (98%). Test patterns from BUILD_RULES.md canonical examples (90%).

patterns_found.name: TanStack Query Hook with Cache Invalidation

key_decisions: bcryptjs over bcrypt (pure JS, no native compilation)
cuid() for Prisma IDs (URL-safe, Prisma-recommended for SQLite)
TanStack Query for React data fetching (validated by Greptacular production use)
tsx watch for server dev (ESM-compatible, zero-config)
Filter state in TanStack Query keys for automatic refetch
prisma db push --force-reset for test DB isolation
